skill.name: global-coding-style
skill.description: Apply consistent coding style guidance for readable names, small focused functions, reduced duplication, formatting, and maintainable source code across common languages.
skill.summary: Guide Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward consistent, maintainable coding style.
skill.author: EIS-ITS

security_audit.summary: AI review found no malicious behavior, scripts, network access, credential access, or executable code. The weak cryptography detections at SKILL.md lines 3 and 24 are false positives from prose, while the path traversal detections at SKILL.md line 29 are a Markdown reference to a parent-directory documentation file. This creates a low marketplace packaging concern, not confirmed malware.

low_findings.title: Parent-Directory Documentation Reference
low_findings.description: SKILL.md references a documentation file through a parent-directory path. This is not executable code and does not itself access the filesystem, but it may make the packaged skill depend on content outside the skill directory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory path is directly visible in a Markdown link. Confidence is below very high because the observed content is documentation guidance, not code that performs filesystem access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Static Path Traversal Pattern in Markdown Link
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer detected parent-directory traversal syntax in a Markdown link. AI review found no executable file access, command execution, or data exfiltration behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The traversal token is present at the reported line, but semantic review shows it is a documentation link rather than an active filesystem operation.

content.value_statement: Inconsistent code style slows reviews and makes maintenance harder. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear guidance for readable, focused, and maintainable code.
actual_capabilities: Promotes meaningful names for variables, functions, classes, and files.
Encourages small focused functions and clearer code structure.
Guides refactoring to reduce duplication and remove dead code.
Supports style review across Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Ruby, Java, and Go.
Recommends use of formatters, linters, and consistent indentation.
limitations: Does not provide language-specific lint rules or configuration files.
Does not replace project style guides or framework conventions.
Does not validate code behavior or runtime correctness.
References guidance outside the packaged skill directory.

use_cases.title: Review a Pull Request for Readability
use_cases.description: Use the skill to identify unclear names, large functions, duplication, and dead code before merging.

use_cases.title: Refactor Legacy Code Safely
use_cases.description: Use the skill to make old code easier to read while preserving behavior and minimizing broad rewrites.

use_cases.title: Create Consistent Team Contributions
use_cases.description: Use the skill to align generated code with shared style expectations across several programming languages.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this file for naming, formatting, duplication, and dead code. Suggest the smallest useful improvements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one source file

prompt_templates.title: Refactor for Clarity
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this function for readability. Keep behavior the same, use clearer names, and split logic only where it helps.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Team Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this code to follow consistent project style. Prefer existing patterns and explain any meaningful tradeoffs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Preparing code for a team codebase

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this module for maintainability risks. Prioritize naming, function size, repeated logic, dead code, and formatter or linter gaps.

output_examples.input: A long function mixes validation, database updates, and response formatting.
output_examples.output: The assistant identifies the separate responsibilities, recommends clearer names, and suggests focused helper functions while preserving behavior.

output_examples.input: A JavaScript file has unused imports, abbreviated variables, and inconsistent formatting.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends removing unused imports, replacing vague abbreviations with meaningful names, and applying the project formatter.

output_examples.input: A pull request adds similar logic in three places.
output_examples.output: The assistant points out the duplication and suggests a shared helper only if it improves clarity and avoids premature abstraction.

best_practices: Provide the relevant file or diff so the assistant can review concrete code.
Ask for behavior-preserving changes when refactoring production code.
Combine this skill with the project linter, formatter, and existing style guide.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for broad rewrites when a focused style pass is enough.
Do not replace project conventions with generic preferences.
Do not treat style feedback as proof that the code is functionally correct.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps improve naming, formatting, function structure, duplication, and general code readability.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: The guidance is general and applies to common languages including Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Ruby, Java, and Go.

faq.question: Does it include formatter configuration?
faq.answer: No. It recommends using formatters and linters, but it does not include configuration files.

faq.question: Can it review existing code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide reviews for clarity, naming, duplication, dead code, and maintainability concerns.

faq.question: Does it guarantee correct behavior?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on style and maintainability, so tests and functional review are still required.

faq.question: Is there any security concern?
faq.answer: AI review found no malicious code. The only concern is a parent-directory documentation link in SKILL.md.
